result.chapter_summary: Chapter 10 presents findings from a robust, eight-year longitudinal study by David Grissmer and colleagues demonstrating that the Core Knowledge curriculum significantly improves reading scores and can eliminate the income-based achievement gap. Hirsch argues that these empirical results challenge the dominant child-centered pedagogy and prove that a shared, knowledge-rich curriculum is the most effective tool for social justice and economic mobility.

claims.text: The results of the Grissmer longitudinal study on Core Knowledge are stronger and clearer than the bulk of studies in reading research.
claims.source_passage: The results were stronger and clearer than the bulk of studies in reading research.

claims.text: The methodology used in the Grissmer longitudinal study was exceptionally punctilious and beyond fault.
claims.source_passage: Nobody could find fault with the punctilious methodology that Grissmer had used.

claims.text: American researchers are under powerful professional pressure not to challenge the child-centered educational status quo.
claims.source_passage: the commentary that the Grissmer research generated, which reveals the powerful pressures American researchers are under not to rock the child-centered boat.

claims.text: The child-centered approach to reading has deeply harmed educational equality and injured the cause of social justice.
claims.source_passage: The child-centered approach to reading has deeply harmed the equality of our early education and has, therefore, deeply injured the cause of social justice.

claims.text: The internet age has exacerbated the financial importance of literacy and the speed of verbal comprehension.
claims.source_passage: Our era has exacerbated the financial importance of literacy. Speed of verbal comprehension and communication and, thence, speediness of decisive action have become more important than before.

claims.text: The chief disadvantage for disadvantaged K-8 pupils, excluding physical malnourishment, is a deficiency in language and knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The chief disadvantage (besides physical malnourishment) of disadvantaged K-8 pupils is a language/knowledge disadvantage.

claims.text: Schooling is unlikely to overcome the literacy gap unless the curriculum specifically arranges to diminish the grapholectic knowledge gap.
claims.source_passage: Unless deliberate arrangements in the school curriculum diminish that grapholectic gap, our schooling is unlikely to overcome the literacy gap between advantaged and disadvantaged children.

claims.text: A carefully sequenced, commonly shared curriculum diminishes the gap between advantaged and disadvantaged children.
claims.source_passage: it’s been demonstrated that a carefully sequenced, commonly shared curriculum will diminish the gap between advantaged and disadvantaged children.

claims.text: A systematic shared-knowledge curriculum enhances the knowledge of advantaged children as well as disadvantaged ones.
claims.source_passage: Of equal importance, that same systematic approach will enhance the grapholectic knowledge of advantaged children as well.

claims.text: The Core Knowledge curriculum has a large beneficial effect on state reading test scores.
claims.source_passage: The results of a long-term study conducted by University of Virginia researchers show that the Core Knowledge curriculum had a large beneficial effect on state reading test scores.

claims.text: The cumulative (K-6) long-term effect of Core Knowledge for all students is approximately 16 percentile points.
claims.source_passage: The cumulative (K-6) long-term effect for all students was approximately 16 percentile points.

claims.text: The Core Knowledge curriculum is capable of eliminating the achievement gap associated with income.
claims.source_passage: These gains were large enough to eliminate the achievement gap associated with income.

claims.text: Core Knowledge curriculum effects are significant for ELA and Science, but were not found to be significant for math in this specific longitudinal study.
claims.source_passage: The TOT effect size was 0.473 across grades 3-6 for English Language Arts... Effects were not significant for math. The overall TOT effect size on the science test... was 0.30, a significant effect.

claims.text: The Core Knowledge curriculum significantly improves English Language Arts (ELA) scores for students who actually experience the curriculum (Treatment on Treated).
claims.source_passage: The TOT effect size was 0.473 across grades 3-6 for English Language Arts, with significant effects at each grade.

claims.text: The Core Knowledge curriculum has a significant positive effect on science achievement in the fifth grade.
claims.source_passage: The overall TOT effect size on the science test administered in Grade 5 was 0.30, a significant effect.

claims.text: The impact of the Core Knowledge curriculum is more pronounced for students in low-income schools than for students in middle- and high-income schools.
claims.source_passage: For the low-income school, effects were larger: 1.299 for English Language Arts and 0.997 (a significant increase) for math.

claims.text: In low-income schools, the Core Knowledge curriculum produces significant gains in mathematics, whereas the effects in higher-income schools are non-significant.
claims.source_passage: For the low-income school the effect size was 0.944 for English Language Arts and 0.735 (a significant effect) for math.

claims.text: Parents react differently to school lottery losses based on the gender of their child, leading to differential attrition that can bias research results.
claims.source_passage: An unexpected result in this study was differential attrition by gender: in short, the parents in this study reacted differently when a boy lost the lottery than when a girl did.

claims.text: Parents are more likely to delay school entry or seek private options for boys who lose school lotteries because they perceive boys as less mature at kindergarten age.
claims.source_passage: Likely because parents view boys as less mature at kindergarten age, they were more likely to keep boys at home for one more year and try the lottery again, or to pay for a private school.

claims.text: The Core Knowledge curriculum systematically builds knowledge and comprehension by integrating topics across seven subject areas from K-8.
claims.source_passage: It centers on a sequence of topics that integrates knowledge from the seven subject areas across K-8 grades to systematically build their knowledge and comprehension of the world.

claims.text: The educational establishment largely ignored the robust longitudinal data supporting the effectiveness of Core Knowledge.
claims.source_passage: This press release was largely ignored, but it was the subject of an earnest report in Education Week.

claims.text: Critiques that the Core Knowledge curriculum is guided by a 'master narrative' are empty rhetoric used to dismiss the curriculum without engagement.
claims.source_passage: I’ll ignore the vague white supremacist implication of the “master narrative” rhetoric as empty rhetoric.

claims.text: The objection that Core Knowledge is 'just one experiment' is a weak reaction from those invested in the educational status quo.
claims.source_passage: I’ll devote the rest of this chapter to the “just one experiment” objection, which assumes or pretends to assume that what is at stake is how effective the particular Core Knowledge curricular sequence is.

claims.text: The fundamental debate in early education is between a shared knowledge structure and the current child-centered, tokenistic, multicultural approach.
claims.source_passage: But what is at stake is the shared knowledge structure of early education vs. our current child-centered, tokenistic, multicultural approach to teaching the grapholect.

claims.text: No credible studies exist that contradict the evidence showing the superiority of teaching shared knowledge (the Kim Transfer Effect).
claims.source_passage: There are zero credible studies that contradict the Kim Transfer Effect showing the superiority of teaching shared knowledge.

claims.text: Evidence from the Grissmer and Kim studies is complemented by a massive, 20-year educational experiment in France involving twelve million pupils.
claims.source_passage: The Grismer and Kim studies complement each other. And so does a huge grammar-school experiment in France conducted with twelve million pupils over twenty years.

claims.text: Educational stakeholders ignore the Grissmer report because they have high intellectual, financial, and emotional investments in child-centered education.
claims.source_passage: With a lot of intellectual and financial, as well as emotional investment in child-centered education, there’s little incentive to pay much attention to or express enthusiasm for the recent Grissmer report

claims.text: Continuing to use child-centered educational methods after the Grissmer report is a nation-harming technical mistake.
claims.source_passage: But, after his report, to continue committing the nation-harming technical mistakes of child-centered education would be unwise.

claims.text: Further studies on shared knowledge will inevitably show the same positive results due to the nature of implicature in human languages.
claims.source_passage: For, right now, we have learned enough about implicature in human languages to assert that further studies will show precisely the same kinds of results.

claims.text: To disambiguate and amplify meaning in language requires specific, relevant background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: To disambiguate and amplify meaning requires specific, relevant background knowledge. Period. End of (that) story.

claims.text: Students who receive shared knowledge are able to gain more shared knowledge on their own.
claims.source_passage: It showed that students who have received shared knowledge gain collectively more shared knowledge on their own.

claims.text: A shared knowledge foundation enables teachers to convey new knowledge effectively to all students in a class, achieving 'far transfer.'
claims.source_passage: their teacher is enabled (as George A. Miller explained) to use that shared foundation to convey new knowledge effectively to all the students in the class (i.e. achieve “far transfer” for all).

claims.text: There are no controlled studies showing that child-centered approaches achieve greater equality than shared knowledge approaches.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, there are no controlled studies showing the contrary— that you can achieve greater equality with child-centered approaches over shared knowledge approaches.

claims.text: Reading scores rise with shared-topic instruction for all pupils, regardless of their home background.
claims.source_passage: They duplicate the Grissmer finding that reading scores will rise with shared-topic instruction for all pupils, regardless of home background.

claims.text: Reading scores fall for all students when individualized reading topics are used, with the greatest decline occurring for disadvantaged students.
claims.source_passage: And that reading scores will fall for all students when individualized reading topics are used. But the declines will be greatest for disadvantaged students.

claims.text: A shared-topic curriculum yields far better reading results than an un-shared-topic curriculum.
claims.source_passage: A shared-topic curriculum (In the Grissmer study, it was the Core Knowledge Sequence.) yields far better reading results than an un-shared-topic curriculum.

claims.text: Reading test results cannot be reliably attributed to a language-arts program alone; they must account for the child's whole curriculum.
claims.source_passage: Results secured by schooling as determined by reading tests can never be reliably attributed solely to a language-arts program... researchers also need to take account of the whole of the child’s curriculum when reporting on progress in reading.

claims.text: A shared-topic curriculum enables further learning and vocabulary building by all students, regardless of home background.
claims.source_passage: Rather, the key variable was the whole shared-topic curriculum that, as the Kim study showed, enables further learning and vocabulary building by all students, regardless of home background.

claims.text: Educational researchers must account for the whole of the child's curriculum, not just SES, when reporting reading progress.
claims.source_passage: But in addition to those SES categories, researchers also need to take account of the whole of the child’s curriculum when reporting on progress in reading.

claims.text: A reading test is a knowledge test in disguise.
claims.source_passage: So, let’s remember the Willingham doctrine about reading tests: “A reading test is a knowledge test in disguise.”

claims.text: Elementary programs that systematically build nationally shared knowledge yield better reading results than programs that do not.
claims.source_passage: An elementary program that systematically builds knowledge shared by literate people in the wider nation will yield better reading results than a program that doesn’t build such publicly shared knowledge.

claims.text: The Core Knowledge shared topic sequence was the primary cause of the superior reading results found in the Grissmer study.
claims.source_passage: Current relevant science shows the Core Knowledge shared topic sequence was the chief cause of the superior reading results found by Grissmer.

claims.text: Common topic knowledge among classmates enables teachers to effect 'far transfer' for all students.
claims.source_passage: Common topic knowledge among the members of a class enables the teacher quickly to effect far transfer for all students in the class – an immense help to fast learning and whole-class equality.

claims.text: The built-in equality principle of shared knowledge is independent of home background and essential for social justice.
claims.source_passage: That built-in equality principle, independent of home background, is immensely important for social justice.

claims.text: The Loi Jospin (1989) required all French public schools to adopt a child-centered approach.
claims.source_passage: In 1989, the loi Jospin was passed, which required thenceforth all the public schools in France to adopt a child-centered approach.

claims.text: The Loi Jospin mandated 'individualized' learning where pupils decide their own learning goals with teachers.
claims.source_passage: The law required French elementary schools to stop teaching a common curriculum and start teaching American style “individualized” learning... 'a contractual pedagogy in which the pupil decides with the teacher the goals of her apprenticeship and its sequence.'

claims.text: French reading scores fell significantly and unequally following the implementation of child-centered learning.
claims.source_passage: Reading scores fell significantly and unequally. The following chart is taken from an official publication of the Ministry.

claims.text: The negative results of the 1989 French reform were significant enough to cause its repeal in 2000.
claims.source_passage: It was repealed in 2000, after its effects were shown to be unfavorable... by 2000, the results were sufficiently negative to cause a repeal of the law.

claims.text: The French study is a carefully controlled, massive, and highly significant longitudinal experiment.
claims.source_passage: It is an experiment of immense value and significance. I call it the French elbow. It was carefully controlled.

claims.text: The French education reform following the 1989 Jospin law constitutes a carefully controlled experiment of immense value and significance regarding educational models.
claims.source_passage: It is an experiment of immense value and significance. I call it the French elbow. It was carefully controlled.

claims.text: The comparison of French reading scores in 1987, 1997, and 2007 is valid because the exams used were identical and the demographic data remained representative.
claims.source_passage: The exam in all three years ’87, ’97, and ’07 was the very same exam, and the representative geographical and sociological data was the same.

claims.text: The French government repealed the Jospin law by 2000 because the educational results were sufficiently negative.
claims.source_passage: (And already, by 2000, the results were sufficiently negative to cause a repeal of the law.)

claims.text: The relative lack of steepness in the 1997 French reading score decline is due to those students receiving traditional schooling in their earliest years before the reform was fully implemented.
claims.source_passage: So the lack of steepness in the 1997 decline is probably owing to their having excellent schooling in their earliest grades before the Shared Knowledge Americanization process was fully in place.

claims.text: The full implementation of 'Americanized' child-centered pedagogy in France resulted in a steep descent in reading scores by 2007.
claims.source_passage: When it was fully in place from earlier years, you get a steep descent. Thus, you get what could be named “the French elbow.”

claims.text: Once the Americanization of the French school system was complete, France began producing educational results similar to those of the United States.
claims.source_passage: When the Americanization process was complete, the French began getting American-style results.

claims.text: The United States is currently ranked twenty-fifth in the Program for International Assessment (PISA) rankings, while the 'Americanized' France fell to twenty-sixth.
claims.source_passage: We are currently twenty-fifth in the Program for International Assessment (PISA) rankings, and the Americanized French fell to twenty-sixth.

claims.text: France's centralized system will likely allow it to improve its educational rankings faster than the United States if it returns to a shared curriculum.
claims.source_passage: If the repeal of the Jospin law leads in to a return to topic commonality in France, my bet is that the centralized French will pull themselves up faster than we will.

claims.text: Lionel Jospin was well-intentioned regarding social justice but lacked necessary knowledge of cognitive psychology when he passed his education law.
claims.source_passage: He was highly concerned with equality and social justice, but he was incompletely informed about cognitive psychology. He could not know that his law would have an ill effect on social justice.

claims.text: Child-centered instruction actively harms the progress of students, particularly those from disadvantaged backgrounds.
claims.source_passage: Child-centered instruction works against the child, especially if she happens to be disadvantaged.

claims.text: Individualized, child-centered education is a primary driver of social inequality in modern schooling.
claims.source_passage: Knowing what we have recently learned about the social inequalities induced by individualized, child-centered education...

claims.text: John Dewey, the leader of the child-centered tradition, would likely abandon his views if he were exposed to modern findings in cognitive science.
claims.source_passage: Our child-centered tradition was led by a man of the left—John Dewey. Were he still with us, I think he would change his view just as Jospin did.

claims.text: Americans should reach a political consensus to abandon child-centered education in order to achieve the ideal of equality of opportunity.
claims.source_passage: I hope Americans of both political leanings will want to make peace on that issue for the sake of the United States and the ideal of equality of opportunity.

claims.text: The Philip Cohen curve, which correlates teen literacy with adult income, is predicted to become an increasingly strong correlation in the internet age.
claims.source_passage: This means that the Philip Cohen curve correlating teen literacy and adult income may become an ever-stronger correlation.

claims.text: The speed of verbal comprehension and communication in the modern era is a direct prerequisite for the speediness of decisive action required in the economy.
claims.source_passage: Speed of verbal comprehension and communication and, thence, speediness of decisive action have become more important than before.

claims.text: Parents in disadvantaged communities like the South Bronx understand that refusing to teach a common culture closes off future economic opportunity for their children.
claims.source_passage: Hence the argument against “imposing the same ‘Anglo’ culture on everybody;” while it sounds like a mini declaration of independence, it is just the opposite. It closes off opportunity. It unintentionally lowers the future income power of grade-schoolers who come from disadvantaged circumstances. Parents in the South Bronx understand this well.

claims.text: Aside from physical malnourishment, the primary deficit facing disadvantaged K-8 pupils is a specific disadvantage in language and background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The chief disadvantage (besides physical malnourishment) of disadvantaged K-8 pupils is a language/knowledge disadvantage.

claims.text: Every American interested in social justice and income equality has a duty to understand the paradox that child-centered curricula entrench existing disadvantages.
claims.source_passage: This paradox needs to be understood by every American interested in social justice and greater income equality.

claims.text: National student gains comparable to those seen in the Grissmer study would move the United States into the top five countries globally in PIRLS reading rankings.
claims.source_passage: national student gains similar to the gains realized in this intervention would place the U.S. among the top five countries.

claims.text: The American educational community responded to the high-quality Bailin-Grafstein book on readability with total silence and a lack of reviews.
claims.source_passage: The main reaction is silence —which was how the American educational community also greeted the Bailin-Grafstein book on readability—silence with no reviews—not even negative reviews

claims.text: To effectively improve literacy and national unity, the weaknesses of 'stand-pat' rhetorical reactions to robust research must be publicly highlighted.
claims.source_passage: The “just one experiment” statement deserves comment because the weakness of such stand-pat reactions should have a bright light shone upon them if we are to elevate our literacy and unity.

claims.text: A shared knowledge foundation enables a teacher to achieve far transfer for all students simultaneously by using the class's collective foundation to convey new knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, it also follows that by means of that collectively shared knowledge, their teacher is enabled (as George A. Miller explained) to use that shared foundation to convey new knowledge effectively to all the students in the class (i.e. achieve “far transfer” for all).

claims.text: The specific starting age for French schooling is three years old, making their longitudinal data particularly relevant for early childhood educational analysis.
claims.source_passage: The French start schooling at age 3. What I’ll call the Jospin experiment in France was a de facto longitudinal study of elementary pupils over a period of twenty years.

evidence.description: An eight-year longitudinal study using a lottery methodology (RCT) at nine oversubscribed Charter schools in Denver, tracking 2,310 applicants (688 selected) from Kindergarten through Grade 6.
U.S. placement (15th out of 50) on the 2016 PIRLS 4th grade Reading/English test used as a benchmark for Core Knowledge gains.
The Philip Cohen curve correlating teen literacy and adult income.
Colorado state-wide PARCC (Partnership for Assessment of Readiness for College and Careers) test scores for ELA and Math from grades 3-6.
A lottery-based longitudinal study of Core Knowledge schools in Colorado using PARCC ELA and Math scores (Grades 3-6) and Science scores (Grade 5).
Analysis of Treatment on Treated (TOT) effect sizes showing 0.473 for ELA and 0.30 for Science in Core Knowledge schools.
Comparison of effect sizes between middle/high-income schools (0.445 ELA) and low-income schools (1.299 ELA) in the Colorado study.
Reporting by Education Week that highlighted dismissive reactions to the Grissmer study from educational experts.
Reference to a 20-year experiment in France involving twelve million pupils as evidence for shared knowledge curricula.
The Grissmer report, a seven-year study by a researcher known for meticulous work, showing better and fairer results from shared-knowledge approaches.
The Kim Transfer Effect, which demonstrates that teaching shared knowledge leads to superior learning outcomes.
French Ministry of Education study (1987-2007) of 12 million pupils over twenty years with meticulous record-keeping of socio-economic origins.
evidence.source_reference: Ministry of Education, France. Lire, écrire, compter: les performances de élèves de CM2 à vingt ans d’intervalle 1987-2007

evidence.description: The Bailin-Grafstein book on readability, which was greeted with silence by the educational community despite its quality.
The Kim study, which demonstrates how shared-topic curricula enable vocabulary building across different backgrounds.
The Grissmer study in Colorado, which showed superior reading results for students using the Core Knowledge Sequence.
Massive French study by the National Ministry of Education (1987, 1997, 2007) using identical test items over 30 years.
Representative geographical and sociological data from the French Ministry of Education publication.
Longitudinal French reading score data (1987, 1997, 2007) showing a specific pattern of decline across all social classes following the 1989 reform.
Program for International Assessment (PISA) rankings showing the US at 25th and France at 26th in reading.
The legislative repeal of the Jospin law by the year 2000 in response to negative educational outcomes.

concepts.author_definition: The specialized, formal vocabulary found in the 'language of the classroom' and in printed books, as opposed to oral, everyday speech.
A statistical measure of the effect of an intervention (like a curriculum) on those students who actually experienced the treatment, rather than just everyone who was intended to receive it.
The dominant pedagogical status quo that prioritizes individual students' interests and general skills over a shared knowledge-rich curriculum.
The effect of the curriculum on all individuals meant to be exposed to the treatment, such as all students who won the lottery, regardless of whether they actually attended the school.
A term used by critics to imply the Core Knowledge curriculum is a biased, tendentious, or potentially white supremacist 'top-down' story of history/knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: A comprehensive or universalized historical account.

concepts.author_definition: A specific research finding or principle showing the superiority of teaching shared knowledge for student achievement and knowledge transfer.
An educational approach where students are taught a common sequence of specific subject matter to provide a foundation for further learning.
A feature of human language where meaning is not fully explicit but depends on shared background knowledge to be disambiguated and understood.
The ability of a teacher to convey new, complex knowledge effectively to all students in a class by building on a shared foundation of existing knowledge.
A curriculum where students study the same topics, enabling collective learning and vocabulary building regardless of home background.
The principle that 'A reading test is a knowledge test in disguise,' meaning reading ability is dependent on specific background knowledge.
The French term for 'child-centered learning,' specifically implemented through the Loi Jospin to replace common curricula with individualized goals.
A pedagogy where the pupil and teacher negotiate learning goals and sequences, as opposed to following a standard curriculum.
The graphical pattern showing a sharp descent in French national reading scores after the 1989 educational reform (the Jospin Law) was fully implemented in early childhood education.
In the French context, the adoption of child-centered, individualized, and skills-focused pedagogical practices similar to those prevalent in the United States.
The educational practice of teaching a shared, specific curriculum to all students in a grade level, as opposed to individualized or fragmented topics.
A correlation between the literacy levels of teenagers and their future income as adults.
The process described in the Kim study where students who have received shared knowledge are able to gain more shared knowledge on their own.
The deficiency in the knowledge of the formal written language (grapholect) that defines early educational disadvantage.
Rhetorical strategies used by the educational establishment to dismiss new, robust research in order to maintain the status quo.

cases.name: Denver Charter Schools Lottery Study
cases.description: A study of 2,310 students who applied to nine charter schools in the Denver area. Using a lottery as a random control, researchers compared the 688 students who received the Core Knowledge curriculum to those who did not. The study found significant long-term gains in ELA and Science and the elimination of the income gap in one low-income school.
key_passages: The six-year study used a lottery methodology: 2,310 students applied to attend one of nine oversubscribed Charter schools that use the Core Knowledge Curriculum
The cumulative (K-6) long-term effect for all students was approximately 16 percentile points.
These gains were large enough to eliminate the achievement gap associated with income.

cases.name: The Colorado Core Knowledge Study
cases.description: A longitudinal study led by Professor Grissmer examining students from kindergarten through grade 6. It used a lottery system to create treatment and control groups and measured performance on PARCC and CTB/McGraw-Hill science tests. The study found large, significant effects in ELA and science, particularly for low-income students, though it encountered methodological challenges regarding gender-based attrition.
key_passages: The treatment began in kindergarten and extended to grade 6. The ELA and Math measures were taken yearly from grades 3 through 6, and the science test was administered once, in Grade 5.
For the low-income school, effects were larger: 1.299 for English Language Arts and 0.997 (a significant increase) for math.
contestable_aspects: The handling of differential attrition by gender
The generalizability of a single-school result for low-income students

cases.name: France (1987-2007)
cases.description: A 20-year longitudinal study conducted by the French Ministry of Education involving 12 million pupils. The study used consistent probes at 10-year intervals to track student performance across different socio-economic backgrounds. It found that reading scores rose with shared instruction and fell when individualized reading topics were introduced, particularly impacting disadvantaged students.
key_passages: From France, the most massive study imaginable has been conducted by the Ministry of Education. It was a twenty-years-long highly controlled study using the same probes at ten-year intervals and representing hundreds of thousands of children from all parts of France

It would not be reasonable or responsible to wave these results aside as just being French. They duplicate the Grissmer finding that reading scores will rise with shared-topic instruction for all pupils, regardless of home background.
contestable_aspects: The characterization of the results as being solely due to the shift from shared to individualized topics.
The transferability of French centralized data results to the American educational system.

thinkers.name: David Grissmer
thinkers.key_idea: Lead researcher of the longitudinal study demonstrating large effect sizes for the Core Knowledge curriculum.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch relies on Grissmer's 'punctilious' methodology to provide the 'robust' empirical proof for his thesis.
key_passages: He is well known for his punctilio, and in fact, he declined to communicate with me at all, over the years, since the Core Knowledge program was the 'treatment' program.

thinkers.name: Philip Cohen
thinkers.key_idea: The correlation between teen literacy levels and adult income (the Cohen curve).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Cohen's data to argue that the economic stakes of literacy are higher in the modern age.
key_passages: This means that the Philip Cohen curve correlating teen literacy and adult income may become an ever-stronger correlation.

thinkers.name: Professor Grissmer
thinkers.key_idea: Conducted the meticulous longitudinal lottery study in Colorado showing the effectiveness of Core Knowledge.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch relies on Grissmer's 'meticulous work' as primary empirical evidence for the chapter's thesis and defends it against dismissive critiques.
key_passages: Let’s not get unsettled by Professor Grissmer’s meticulous work.

thinkers.name: E. D. Hirsch
thinkers.key_idea: Founder of the Core Knowledge Foundation; developed the curriculum centered on a sequence of topics to systematically build knowledge.
thinkers.engagement: The author cites his own research and the curriculum he founded as the basis for the intervention being studied.
key_passages: The Core Knowledge Curriculum is based on the ideas and research of E. D. Hirsch, founder of the Core Knowledge Foundation.

thinkers.name: George A. Miller
thinkers.key_idea: Shared knowledge foundations enable teachers to convey new knowledge effectively to all students (achieving far transfer).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Miller's cognitive psychology framework to explain why the empirical results of the Kim study occur.
key_passages: their teacher is enabled (as George A. Miller explained) to use that shared foundation to convey new knowledge effectively to all the students in the class

thinkers.name: Daniel Willingham
thinkers.key_idea: A reading test is effectively a knowledge test because comprehension depends on domain-specific knowledge.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch adopts this as the 'Willingham doctrine' and uses it as a foundational principle to explain why Core Knowledge schools succeed on reading tests.
key_passages: So, let’s remember the Willingham doctrine about reading tests: “A reading test is a knowledge test in disguise.”

thinkers.name: James Kim
thinkers.key_idea: A shared-topic curriculum enables vocabulary building and 'near transfer' of knowledge.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Kim's work to explain the psychological mechanism behind the success of shared-topic curricula.
key_passages: Rather, the key variable was the whole shared-topic curriculum that, as the Kim study showed, enables further learning and vocabulary building by all students.

thinkers.name: Lionel Jospin
thinkers.key_idea: Passed a 1989 education law aimed at social justice and equality, emphasizing a more child-centered approach.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch characterizes Jospin as well-meaning but 'incompletely informed about cognitive psychology,' arguing his reforms unintentionally damaged social justice.
key_passages: Lionel Jospin (the minister of education when the law was passed) is a distinguished socialist... He was highly concerned with equality and social justice, but he was incompletely informed about cognitive psychology.

thinkers.name: John Dewey
thinkers.key_idea: Leader of the American child-centered educational tradition.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch argues that Dewey was a 'man of the left' focused on equality, but that his child-centered methods actually work against disadvantaged children. Hirsch speculates that Dewey would change his mind if presented with modern data.
key_passages: Our child-centered tradition was led by a man of the left—John Dewey. Were he still with us, I think he would change his view just as Jospin did.

actors.name: David Grissmer
actors.context: Professor and lead researcher at the University of Virginia known for rigorous methodology.

actors.name: Daniel Willingham
actors.context: Cognitive scientist and co-author of the Core Knowledge study.

actors.name: Parents in the South Bronx
actors.context: Cited as understanding the importance of shared knowledge for economic opportunity better than child-centered theorists.

actors.name: Education Week
actors.context: The publication that reported on the Grissmer study and captured the dismissive reactions from the educational field.

actors.name: Core Knowledge Foundation
actors.context: The organization providing the curriculum and professional development used in the 2,000 schools mentioned.

actors.name: French Ministry of Education
actors.context: Conducted a massive 20-year study on student performance that supports the shared-knowledge model.

actors.name: American educational community
actors.context: Accused of greeting evidence-based shared-knowledge research with silence and dismissal due to ideological investment in child-centered pedagogy.

actors.name: French Ministry of Education
actors.context: Conducted the longitudinal research and official testing (1987-2007) that showed the decline of French reading scores.

actors.name: Lionel Jospin
actors.context: French politician associated with the 1989 law that introduced child-centered education.

actors.name: Lionel Jospin
actors.context: Former French Minister of Education and Prime Minister responsible for the 1989 education reform.

actors.name: John Dewey
actors.context: Philosophical leader of the American child-centered education movement.

actors.name: PISA (Program for International Assessment)
actors.context: Organization providing international educational rankings used to compare US and French performance.

objections_raised.objection: A common shared curriculum is an attempt at 'imposing the same "Anglo" culture on everybody.'
objections_raised.response: This argument is a false 'mini declaration of independence' that actually closes off opportunity and lowers future income power for disadvantaged students.

objections_raised.objection: The Core Knowledge curriculum is guided by a 'master narrative' (implying it is tendentious or white supremacist).
objections_raised.source: Unnamed expert quoted in Education Week
objections_raised.response: This is vague, empty rhetoric used to dismiss the curriculum without engaging with its actual details or effectiveness.

objections_raised.objection: The Colorado study is 'just one experiment' and its results might not be replicable.
objections_raised.response: This 'stand-pat' reaction ignores that what is at stake is the fundamental structure of schooling; furthermore, massive supporting studies (Kim and the French experiment) complement these findings.

objections_raised.objection: The Core Knowledge model is based on 'just one experiment' or study.
objections_raised.response: The Grissmer report is not about a single proprietary program; it reveals the superiority of the shared-knowledge variable, which is supported by the Kim study and a 20-year French study of 12 million students.

objections_raised.objection: The Grissmer study is just a single relevant experiment or an outlier.
objections_raised.response: The evidence for the principle of shared knowledge curricula is overwhelming and has been documented in other works like 'Why Knowledge Matters.'

objections_raised.objection: The 1997 French reading score decline was not as steep as the 2007 decline.
objections_raised.response: The 1997 cohort received 'excellent schooling' (shared knowledge) in their earliest grades (starting at age 3) before the Jospin reform was fully established, whereas the 2007 cohort was exposed to the reform from the start.

cross_chapter_refs.reference_text: The evidence on that basic principle is overwhelming and outlined in my book, Why Knowledge Matters.
